perturbation.original_text: 10. This agreement must be in accordance with, and gove rned in all respects by the laws of Spain.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. This agreement must be in accordance with, and gove rned in all respects by the laws of [COUNTRY].
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to a generic '[COUNTRY]' removes the legal certainty provided by specifying Spanish law. Spanish law offers consumer protections that would be applicable when the agreement covers the exchange of personal data of consumers. If this agreement applies to consumers in Spain, removing the reference to Spanish law and replacing it with a vague '[COUNTRY]' could violate GDPR protections for consumers in Spain.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 6(1) GDPR requires a lawful basis for processing personal data, such as consent or a contract. By obscuring the governing law, it becomes unclear whether the data processing aspects of the agreement comply with GDPR, potentially violating consumer data protection rights.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 6 GDPR – Lawfulness of processing - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Processing shall be lawful only if and to the extent that at least one of the following applies: the data subject has given consent to the processing of his or her personal data for one or more specific purposes; processing is necessary for the performance of a contract to which the data subject is party or in order to take steps at the request of the data subject prior to entering into a contract; processing is necessary for compliance with a legal obligation to which the controller is subject; processing is necessary in order to protect the vital interests of the data subject or of another natural person; processing is necessary for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest or in the exercise of official authority vested in the controller; processing is necessary for the purposes of the legitimate interests pursued by the controller or by a third party, except where such interests are overridden by the interests or fundamental rights and freedoms of the data subject which require protection of personal data, in particular where the data subject is a child. Point (f) of the first subparagraph shall not apply to processing carried out by public authorities in the performance of their tasks. Member States may maintain or introduce more specific provisions to adapt the application of the rules of this Regulation with regard to processing for compliance with points (c) and (e) of paragraph 1 by determining more precisely specific requirements for the processing and other measures to ensure lawful and fair processing including for other specific processing situations as provided for in Chapter IX . 1 The basis for the processing referred to in point (c) and (e) of paragraph 1 shall be laid down by: Union law; or Member State law to which the controller is subject. 2 The purpose of the processing shall be determined in that legal basis or, as regards the processing referred to in point (e) of paragraph 1, shall be necessary for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest or in the exercise of official authority vested in the controller. 3 That legal basis may contain specific provisions to adapt the application of rules of this Regulation, inter alia: the general conditions governing the lawfulness of processing by the controller; the types of data which are subject to the processing; the data subjects concerned; the entities to, and the purposes for which, the personal data may be disclosed; the purpose limitation; storage periods; and processing operations and processing procedures, including measures to ensure lawful and fair processing such as those for other specific processing situations as provided for in Chapter IX . 4 The Union or the Member State law shall meet an objective of public interest and be proportionate to the legitimate aim pursued. Where the processing for a purpose other than that for which the personal data have been collected is not based on the data subject’s consent or on a Union or Member State law which constitutes a necessary and proportionate measure in a democratic society to safeguard the objectives referred to in Article 23 (1), the controller shall, in order to ascertain whether processing for another purpose is compatible with the purpose for which the personal data are initially collected, take into account, inter alia: any link between the purposes for which the personal data have been collected and
perturbation.justification: The omission of a specific governing law (Spain) and its replacement with a generic '[COUNTRY]' introduces a legal contradiction. This is because, as the perturbation explanation states, Spanish law offers consumer data protection under GDPR when the agreement involves processing personal data of consumers in Spain. The Law Explanation further emphasizes that Article 6(1) GDPR requires a lawful basis for processing personal data. By obscuring the governing law, it becomes unclear whether the agreement adheres to GDPR, which could lead to a violation of consumer data protection rights. The scraped law snippet supports this, highlighting that processing personal data must be lawful and based on conditions like consent or contract necessity. The change from Spanish law to a generic '[COUNTRY]' thus undermines the legal certainty required for lawful data processing under GDPR, creating a contradiction by omitting a legally mandated consumer protection.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Disclosure: A party (the “ disclosing Party ”) may, in connection with the Business Discussions, disclose to the other Party informatio n, including, without limitation, oral, written, graphical or electronic information and all compilations, analyses, notes, copies prepared, generated or delivered ther e from, which is identified as “confidential information” or “confidential busines s information” (the “Confidential Business Information ”) by the Disclosing Party at the time of disclosure to the receiving party (the “ Receiving Party ”). The fact the Business Discussions are occurring and any negotiations, ter ms or facts related to the Business Discussions are Confidential Business Info rmation.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Disclosure: A party (the “ disclosing Party ”) may, in connection with the Business Discussions, disclose to the other Party informatio n, including, without limitation, oral, written, graphical or electronic information and all compilations, analyses, notes, copies prepared, generated or delivered ther e from, which is identified as “confidential information” or “confidential busines s information” (the “Confidential Business Information ”) by the Disclosing Party at the time of disclosure to the receiving party (the “ Receiving Party ”).
perturbation.explanation: By removing the sentence 'The fact the Business Discussions are occurring and any negotiations, ter ms or facts related to the Business Discussions are Confidential Business Info rmation,' the agreement becomes vague about what constitutes confidential information, which could violate trade secret laws if the discussions involve trade secrets. Under the US Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), reasonable measures to protect trade secrets are required.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. By omitting the specific clause that designates the business discussions themselves as confidential, the agreement arguably fails to provide adequate protection, potentially jeopardizing trade secret status.

(2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The omission of the sentence defining the business discussions as confidential could lead to a failure to protect trade secrets, potentially violating the DTSA. The original contract explicitly defined 'the fact the Business Discussions are occurring and any negotiations, ter ms or facts related to the Business Discussions' as Confidential Business Information. The changed text removes this explicit designation, weakening the protection of such information. According to the LLM explanation, 'the DTSA requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets' and omitting the clause designating discussions as confidential 'arguably fails to provide adequate protection, potentially jeopardizing trade secret status.' Scraped Law Snippet 1, 18 USC 1836, pertains to civil proceedings related to trade secret misappropriation, implying the importance of protecting trade secrets. While the snippet doesn't explicitly define 'reasonable measures,' the act's existence suggests that contracts must take measures to ensure confidentiality. By removing the sentence, the contract becomes less protective, creating a higher risk of trade secret misappropriation and thus a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4. The restrictions as to the use and disclosure set o ut above shall not apply to: a. any of the confidential information which is or bec omes published otherwise than by unauthorised publication in breac h of this agreement. b. or any of the Confidential Information which is sho wn by written or other tangible evidence to have been known to the recipie nt prior to the date of the disclosure hereunder. c. or any of the confidential information which is law fully acquired by the recipient from an independent source having a bona fida right to disclose the same. d. or any Confidential Information which as demonstrat e by tangible evidence is independently developed by an employee of the re cipient who has not had access to any of the Information disclosed to the r ecipient by the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. The restrictions as to the use and disclosure set o ut above shall not apply to: a. any of the confidential information which is or bec omes published otherwise than by unauthorised publication in breac h of this agreement. b. or any of the Confidential Information which is sho wn by written or other tangible evidence to have been known to the recipie nt prior to the date of the disclosure hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Removing exceptions 'c' and 'd' concerning information lawfully acquired from an independent source or independently developed could create an overly broad restriction on using information, potentially violating antitrust laws. This restriction could prevent a receiving party from using publicly available information or internally developed knowledge, hindering competition. If the business discussions relate to pharmaceutical patents, for example, such a broad confidentiality provision could be problematic.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 1 of the Sherman Act prohibits contracts or conspiracies that restrain trade. By overly restricting the use of independently acquired or developed information, the modified clause could be interpreted as an unreasonable restraint on trade, especially if it prevents the receiving party from competing effectively.

Every person who shall make any contract or engage in any combination or conspiracy hereby declared to be illegal shall be
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes exceptions to the confidentiality agreement, potentially restricting the use of independently acquired or developed information. This could be seen as an unreasonable restraint on trade, especially if it hinders competition. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 15 USC 1, states that "Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal." The omission of clauses allowing use of independently obtained or developed information directly contradicts this law, as it could be interpreted as an illegal restraint of trade. The modification removes core legal obligations, making the contradiction direct and serious from a legal perspective.

perturbation.original_text: 6. The obligations of confidentiality in this agreemen t shall cease when both parties give written notification of its termination, or 5 years after the date hereof, whichever is the sooner.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. The obligations of confidentiality in this agreemen t shall continue indefinitely.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the termination clause (5 years or written notification) creates a perpetual confidentiality obligation. This could be deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade and unenforceable, especially if the information involved eventually becomes publicly available or loses its competitive advantage. This could contradict California's Business and Professions Code regarding trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: While CUTSA protects trade secrets, indefinite confidentiality obligations can be challenged as unreasonable, especially if the information loses its trade secret status over time. A perpetual restriction is more likely to be deemed an unenforceable restraint on trade under California law.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The original contract had a termination clause for confidentiality obligations, either after 5 years or upon written notification. The changed text removes this termination clause, making the confidentiality obligations continue indefinitely. The Law Explanation suggests this could be an unreasonable restraint on trade, particularly if the information loses its trade secret status. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 primarily defines terms like "Improper means" and "Misappropriation" within the context of trade secrets, the Law Explanation raises concerns about indefinite confidentiality obligations conflicting with California law regarding trade secrets and restraint of trade. Thus, removing the termination clause creates a potentially unenforceable obligation, creating a contradiction.
